structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records from a single healthcare system spanning 3 years (36 months). All patient visits with complete demographic, clinical, diagnostic, and readmission outcome data. Records are extracted from months 1–36 of the dataset.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge (yes/no). Defined prospectively: a patient is labeled positive if they return to any hospital in the system within 30 days of the initial visit discharge date. Outcome is determined from hospital admission records and is not subject to missingness.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by admission urgency and comorbidity burden. Training: months 1–24 (66% of data). Validation: months 25–30 (17% of data). Test: months 31–36 (17% of data). Justification: temporal split prevents data leakage—the RNN's sequential modeling would benefit unfairly from seeing future visit patterns if random split were used. Stratified by urgency (emergency vs. planned admission) and Charlson Comorbidity Index tertiles to ensure both train and validation sets represent the full spectrum of clinical acuity, which affects readmission risk independently of the model architecture.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (feature scalers, sequence encoders, imputation strategies) are fit on the training set only and then applied identically to validation and test sets. This includes: (1) standardization of continuous clinical variables (mean and std computed on training only), (2) fitting of categorical encoders for diagnoses and procedures on training vocabulary only, (3) the sequence padding and tokenization scheme derived from training sequences only. No information from validation or test data informs preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Logistic regression baseline features: age, sex, race/ethnicity, Charlson Comorbidity Index, number of chronic diagnoses (≥3, ≥5), insurance type, prior hospitalizations in past 12 months, length of stay (current visit), primary diagnosis category (ICD-10 grouping). All features use raw EHR values or standard clinical aggregations without lookahead. RNN features: sequences of patient encounters constructed as follows: (1) for each patient, construct a chronological sequence of visits in the 12 months prior to the index visit, with a maximum sequence length of 50 visits; (2) each visit is encoded as a fixed-length vector including: admission date (days since previous visit, binned), primary diagnosis (one-hot encoded from top 100 ICD-10 codes, others grouped as 'other'), admission type (emergency/urgent/planned), length of stay (days), procedures performed (aggregated count by category), and discharge disposition. (3) Sequences shorter than 50 visits are left-padded with zero vectors. This encoding preserves temporal order and visit-level detail. Both model feature sets exclude: future diagnoses, procedures, or readmissions after the index visit; any information from post-discharge follow-up; repeat count of the outcome variable.
structured_choices.model: Bidirectional LSTM (2 layers, 128 hidden units per layer, dropout=0.3 between layers, Adam optimizer, binary cross-entropy loss). The model is trained to convergence on the training set using early stopping on validation set performance (metric: recall at 80% precision threshold). This architecture is chosen because bidirectional LSTMs effectively capture temporal dependencies in visit sequences and are standard for sequential clinical data.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression with L2 regularization, trained on the same training set using 5-fold cross-validation (nested within training data only) to select regularization strength C via grid search on average validation recall. The baseline receives the same preprocessing effort: feature scaling, one-hot encoding of categorical variables, and imputation of missing values via median (continuous) or mode (categorical). The baseline uses the feature set described above, which is a strict subset of raw EHR fields, and does not include engineered sequence representations. Both models are tuned for the same primary metric (recall) to ensure fair comparison. Logistic regression represents the current standard-of-care approach to readmission prediction in many health systems.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Recall at a precision threshold of 80%. Justification: stakeholders prioritize identifying patients at risk of readmission (reducing false negatives) to enable early interventions. A recall-focused metric reflects this operational goal. The 80% precision threshold is set to ensure the model does not overwhelm clinical teams with false alarms—any patient flagged for intervention should be readmission-positive with at least 0.8 probability. Pairs with precision to define actionability. At this threshold, we measure sensitivity (recall) to quantify how many true readmission cases are caught.
secondary_metrics: AUPRC (area under precision-recall curve, appropriate given imbalance)
F1-score at the 80% precision threshold (balance sensitivity and specificity)
Calibration: expected calibration error (ECE) binned into deciles (ensures predicted probabilities match observed rates)
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning for both models is performed using the validation set (months 25–30). For the LSTM: early stopping monitors validation recall at 80% precision; the model is saved at the epoch with highest validation recall under that constraint. For logistic regression: 5-fold cross-validation within the training set selects C, then the model is retrained on all training data with the selected C and evaluated on the validation set. The validation set is used to select the decision threshold (precision=80%) for both models. The test set is held untouched until final evaluation.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 31–36) is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection and threshold tuning are complete. Both the LSTM and logistic regression are applied to the test set using their final, fixed parameters. Test set performance on all primary and secondary metrics is reported. No iteration or refitting occurs after test evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Stratified split on Charlson Comorbidity Index and admission urgency ensures both models are evaluated on patients of comparable baseline acuity. Post-hoc analysis: performance is stratified by age group (18–40, 41–65, 66+), comorbidity burden (low/medium/high CCI), and primary diagnosis category. If performance gaps between models vary substantially across strata, this indicates the models may be solving different subproblems rather than the sequential architecture universally improving prediction. Analysis of patient subgroups with limited visit history (<5 prior visits in 12 months) ensures the RNN does not rely on artifactually strong performance in high-volume patients while failing in low-visit populations. Scope: this design will establish whether the RNN achieves higher recall than logistic regression on the test set at 80% precision and whether performance is consistent across clinically meaningful subgroups. It will not establish causality, generalizability to other health systems, or appropriateness of the 30-day readmission window (which is domain-determined, not data-determined).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

This experiment uses electronic health records from a single healthcare system spanning 36 months of patient encounters. The dataset contains approximately 200,000 visits with complete demographic, clinical, diagnostic, and outcome information. The outcome is defined prospectively: a patient is labeled positive for readmission if they return to any hospital in the system within 30 days of discharge from the index visit. This label is determined from institutional admission records and is not subject to missing data.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

To preserve temporal integrity and prevent leakage, we employ a temporal split: training data spans months 1–24 (66%), validation data covers months 25–30 (17%), and the held-out test set covers months 31–36 (17%). Within each split, we stratify by admission urgency (emergency vs. planned/urgent) and Charlson Comorbidity Index tertiles. This stratification ensures both models are evaluated on clinically representative populations with balanced acuity distributions across all three sets. The temporal structure is critical: the RNN will exploit sequential patterns in visit history, and a random split would allow it to observe future visit information relative to the training window, creating an unfair advantage. The stratified approach controls for baseline acuity, a known confound influencing readmission risk independent of model architecture.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Logistic regression features** (baseline): age (years), sex, race/ethnicity, Charlson Comorbidity Index score, presence of ≥3 and ≥5 chronic diagnoses, insurance type, number of hospitalizations in the prior 12 months, current length of stay, and primary diagnosis category (ICD-10 grouping). All features use raw or standardly aggregated EHR values with no lookahead.

**RNN features** (treatment): for each patient, we construct a sequence of up to 50 prior visits within the 12 months preceding the index visit, in chronological order. Each visit is encoded as a fixed-length vector containing: (1) days since the prior visit (binned), (2) primary diagnosis (one-hot encoded from the 100 most frequent ICD-10 codes; others pooled as 'other'), (3) admission type (categorical: emergency/urgent/planned), (4) length of stay (continuous), (5) aggregated procedure counts by category. Sequences shorter than 50 visits are left-padded with zeros. This representation preserves temporal order and visit-level clinical detail.

**Preprocessing fit point**: All transformers—including standardization of continuous variables, categorical encoders, and sequence tokenization—are fit on the training set only. Parameters (mean, std, vocabulary) derived from training data are then applied identically to validation and test data. This prevents any leakage of test or validation information into preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Treatment model**: A bidirectional LSTM with 2 stacked layers (128 hidden units each), dropout of 0.3 between layers, trained with Adam optimizer and binary cross-entropy loss. This architecture is standard for sequential clinical data and captures both forward and backward dependencies in visit sequences.

**Baseline model**: Logistic regression with L2 regularization (C parameter tuned via 5-fold cross-validation nested within the training set on validation recall). The baseline receives identical preprocessing effort and tuning intensity as the LSTM. Logistic regression represents the current clinical standard for readmission prediction and provides a fair, interpretable comparison.

### 5. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (months 25–30) is used to: (1) select hyperparameters (regularization strength for logistic regression via nested CV; early stopping epoch and decision threshold for the LSTM), and (2) determine the decision threshold for both models such that predicted positive cases achieve 80% precision. The validation set is never used for final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Recall at 80% precision. This aligns with the stakeholder objective: maximizing detection of readmission-risk patients while maintaining clinical actionability (at least 80% of flagged patients are true positives). Recall quantifies sensitivity—how many patients who will truly be readmitted are identified.

**Secondary metrics**: (1) AUROC to assess discrimination across all thresholds, (2) AUPRC to account for class imbalance, (3) F1-score at the 80% precision threshold, (4) Expected Calibration Error (ECE) in decile bins to ensure predicted probabilities match observed readmission rates.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Final Reporting

The test set (months 31–36) is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete. Both models are applied with fixed parameters derived from training and validation phases. Primary and secondary metrics are computed on the test set, and performance is stratified by age group, comorbidity burden, and diagnosis category to check for consistency and detect subgroup differences. Patients with fewer than 5 prior visits in the 12-month window are analyzed separately to ensure the RNN does not rely on high-volume patient artifacts.

### 8. Scope and Confounds

This design establishes whether the RNN achieves higher recall than logistic regression at 80% precision on held-out test data and whether this advantage is robust across clinically meaningful subgroups. Stratification by comorbidity and urgency controls for baseline acuity as a confound. Post-hoc subgroup analysis detects any performance heterogeneity. The design does not establish generalizability to other health systems, causality, or the optimality of the 30-day readmission window.
